title: linux-desktop: Option for FreeBSD VM
body:
### Feature request description

Currently, `podman machine` provides VM support for macOS and Windows using QEMU and Fedora CoreOS. However, there is no official support for running a FreeBSD-based VM on **Linux Desktop**.  

With projects like https://github.com/cbsd/cbsd/blob/89cf09b2327e2de6b9fd68a401d8d51a204aa977/share/docs/general/cbsd_oci.md, https://github.com/samuelkarp/runj/blob/883bf5f8c890b0f76b3c82c160de039a0323ae27/README.md and FreeBSD’s evolving OCI compatibility, having a FreeBSD-based VM for `podman machine` would allow users to run FreeBSD OCI containers natively without relying on full virtualization (e.g., KVM).  


### Suggest potential solution

- Implement a **FreeBSD VM backend** for `podman machine` on Linux, similar to how Fedora CoreOS is used on macOS/Windows.  
- Use **QEMU** (or potentially Bhyve, if feasible) to provide a FreeBSD guest VM.  
- Provide a **pre-built FreeBSD image** with necessary tools for running OCI workloads, similar to Fedora CoreOS.  


### Have you considered any alternatives?

- Running FreeBSD as a full VM via KVM manually. This works but lacks Podman integration.  
- Using an **OCI-compatible FreeBSD runtime** like `runj`. However, this does not fully replace the need for a FreeBSD VM in Podman.  

### Additional context

- FreeBSD's increasing support for OCI (such as `runj`) suggests that a FreeBSD VM in Podman could be a useful addition.  
- Having official support for a FreeBSD VM would allow for better integration and testing of FreeBSD-based OCI workloads within Podman. 

Would the team be open to adding support for this? What would be the best approach—leveraging KVM, QEMU, Bhyve, or another solution?

comments.body:
see https://github.com/containers/podman/pull/19939

My position has not changed. This seems like a maintenance burden for us maintainers without much benifit.
Machine is already complex enough I am not interested in making it even more complicated by supporting an entire new OS with different config mechanisms.

While it would be nice to be able to run containers on FreeBSD (both common Linux ones, and regular FreeBSD jails) - it is not obvious why one would like to run them on another OS (like Mac/Win/Linux) instead of the existing Podman Machine OS?

There was a similar discussion in Lima (https://github.com/lima-vm/lima/issues/1508), where you can run Podman on a regular OS instead of the custom OS (if you want) but it was struggling for when you would want to use a FreeBSD VM instead of a Linux VM.

Most of it is POSIX-based, so the differences are minimal; David wrapped them in just 100 LOC https://github.com/containers/podman/pull/19939/files#diff-4a3677ed5171641eba40e911f19bbf5970550a9e1ce75a30959e6c8032b2e39c. If this is all it takes to enable FreeBSD containers leverage the OCI interface via the Podman UX, it becomes a highly valuable solution for maintaining consistency across environments where organizations have heavily invested in containerized infrastructure. I understand @Luap99's perspective on distinguishing "official" vs. "community" supported host OS. With some extensibility work, this could be designed more efficiently. That said, PR #19939 doesn’t necessarily need to be blocked on it, IMO.

Except that PR does the absolute bare minimum so why use podman machine then. I don't need to repeat my points from the PR here. If you want to run a freebsd image you can use a generic virtual machine manager already.

Generic VM without containers? That sounds like going backwards and unrelated to the discussion at hand. As mentioned in top post OCI support has came a long way in FreeBSD (number of tools already support freebsd OCI, I've shared links). This feature request is about enabling it through podman.

There is nothing stopping you from working within what has provided already is there?  I could envision someone creating a BSD virtual machine image that correlates to our machine image.  Ignition would need to be present.  As would a number of services and abilities to do volume mounts and so forth.  then this image would simply be used with `podman machine init --image /path/to/your/custom_image`.
